title: Nev-AD-a

jokes.text: I'm having reservations about the reservations.
jokes.setup: Selina wants to remove Native American precincts from the recount list and says she has reservations about it.
jokes.punchline: The double meaning of 'reservations' (doubts + Native American reservations) lands in a single tight phrase, with the added dark edge of disenfranchising indigenous voters.
jokes.explanation: This is exceptional Veep wordplay — perfectly economical (six words), politically dark, and utterly Selina. The pun is set up and paid off in the same breath. It earns its dark edge because it's revealing character (Selina's casual amorality) rather than just being edgy for shock value. Comparable in economy to the 8.0 anchor and arguably tighter.

jokes.text: I'm at Walter Reed. I'm meeting troops and shaking God only knows what.
jokes.setup: Selina is performing a patriotic duty — visiting wounded veterans at Walter Reed.
jokes.punchline: Her dread and disgust at the physical reality of the handshaking undercuts the noble framing; she's more worried about what she's touching than the sacrifice of the troops.
jokes.explanation: Pure Selina — her self-interest and physical squeamishness overriding any genuine empathy. 'God only knows what' is a superb understatement that lets the audience fill in the horrific blanks. Very character-specific.

jokes.text: She's changed her mind more times than a frickin' child molester at Disneyland.
jokes.setup: Staff member is exasperated by Selina's constant precinct changes for the recount.
jokes.punchline: The analogy is shockingly transgressive — comparing presidential indecision to a predator at a theme park. The darkness of the image is the joke.
jokes.explanation: Classic Veep transgression — it earns a big shocked laugh but relies partly on the shock of the subject matter rather than pure craft. Ben's exasperation is well-characterized. The simile is vivid and specific enough to elevate it above pure shock value.

jokes.text: Yeah, and if you miss the deadline, find a rattlesnake and shove that up your dick hole 'cause it's a lot more fun than what I'll do to you. And drive safe.
jokes.setup: Ben is giving urgent instructions to a courier about filing papers at the courthouse on time.
jokes.punchline: The escalating threat peaks at grotesque imagery, then deflates with the mundane polite sign-off 'And drive safe.'
jokes.explanation: The structure is near-perfect: vivid grotesque escalation punctured by the blandest possible sign-off. 'And drive safe' is the entire joke and it's excellently timed. Very Veep — the civility of 'drive safe' after 'shove a rattlesnake up your dick hole' captures the show's tone perfectly.

jokes.text: Oh, God, Maddox is probably going to study the effects of legalized prostitution on his dick.
jokes.setup: Ben mentions Maddox is flying in to Nevada, which has legalized prostitution.
jokes.punchline: Selina immediately assumes the worst about Maddox's intentions with trademark caustic wit.
jokes.explanation: Functional Veep insult. The phrasing ('study the effects... on his dick') has a clinical mock-academic quality that elevates it slightly above a straight insult, but it's not among the episode's best.

jokes.text: Well, maybe we can get some six-year-old Asian kid to fix it.
jokes.setup: Staff are struggling to get the video connection to Amy working.
jokes.punchline: Selina's casually racist 'solution' reveals her obliviousness while trying to appear tech-savvy.
jokes.explanation: Selina's casual bigotry is on-brand but this particular gag relies more on the transgression of the stereotype than on any structural craft. Functional for characterization.

jokes.text: Mike reveals baby will be named Ellen after 'Wendy's mom. Actually, her stepmom. She has a difficult relation...' — then cuts off mid-sentence.
jokes.setup: Selina politely asks when Mike's baby is coming; Mike enthusiastically over-shares about the name.
jokes.punchline: Selina's complete lack of interest cuts him off — 'misunderstood your level of interest, sorry' — before he can finish explaining the complicated family dynamics.
jokes.explanation: Classic cringe beat — Mike's earnest over-sharing cut dead by the social reality of Selina's total indifference. The abrupt cut-off is well-structured and the next line ('misunderstood your level of interest') is a perfect button.

jokes.text: Seriously, I'm more Hispanic than she is. You know? Where's she from, Santo Connecticut?
jokes.setup: The team is watching O'Brien's VP pick Montez on screen, questioning her Hispanic authenticity.
jokes.punchline: 'Santo Connecticut' — a portmanteau of 'Santo Domingo' and 'Connecticut' — is a sharp coinage mocking the idea of an assimilated, privileged 'Hispanic' politician.
jokes.explanation: 'Santo Connecticut' is an elegant, original coinage that packages a political observation into a single invented place name. Very economical wit.

jokes.text: She summers in Vinyardo Del Martha. / She's from El New Hampshire.
jokes.setup: Amy has started a riff mocking Montez's supposedly inauthentic Hispanic heritage with 'Santo Connecticut.'
jokes.punchline: Ben and Selina escalate the game with their own invented Spanish-ified WASP locations, each funnier than the last.
jokes.explanation: This escalating riff is one of the episode's highlights. 'Vinyardo Del Martha' (Martha's Vineyard) and 'El New Hampshire' are perfectly constructed portmanteaus. The escalation structure is elegant — three writers riffing, each building on the previous. The jokes reward attention. Selina's laugh after 'El New Hampshire' signals it landed even in-world.

jokes.text: He makes a lot of sense. [beat] All right, so they've got a Secretary of State and what do I have? Harpo, Chico, and Shito.
jokes.setup: O'Brien's recount expert Whitman gives a confident, reasonable-sounding statement. Ben says 'He makes a lot of sense.'
jokes.punchline: Selina pivots from acknowledging the competence of the opposition to immediately insulting her own team with a Marx Brothers riff that adds 'Shito' as a vulgar third brother.
jokes.explanation: Exceptional Veep joke. The Marx Brothers reference (Harpo, Chico) sets a culturally specific comedic frame, then 'Shito' — the invented scatological fourth Marx Brother — lands as a perfect subversion. It's high-low comedy at its best: erudite enough to require the Marx Brothers reference, crude enough to get the gut laugh. Highly quotable, character-specific to Selina's contempt, and economically delivered.

jokes.text: Also an accomplished ballroom dancer.
jokes.setup: Selina is pitching Bob Bradley as her recount expert, listing his impressive credentials: served in more administrations than any man alive.
jokes.punchline: She adds 'Also an accomplished ballroom dancer' with the same gravitas — a completely irrelevant credential delivered with full sincerity.
jokes.explanation: A clean non-sequitur delivered with deadpan authority. The comedy is in the utter confidence with which an irrelevant fact is treated as a qualifying credential. Economy is superb — five words.

jokes.text: He's our Whitman hit man.
jokes.setup: The team decides to bring in Bob Bradley to counter O'Brien's recount expert James Whitman.
jokes.punchline: 'Whitman hit man' — an internal rhyme that makes a political strategy sound like a hip-hop threat.
jokes.explanation: A fun rhyming couplet. Not deep but very quotable. Selina's instinct to turn everything into a catchphrase is well-captured.

jokes.text: Maybe they can find out what some of these people are doing.
jokes.setup: Staff informs Selina that Chinese hackers have breached White House employee files.
jokes.punchline: Rather than being alarmed, Selina suggests the hack might be useful for her own internal management — implying she doesn't trust or know her own staff.
jokes.explanation: Sharp reversal — a national security breach reframed as a personnel management tool. Captures Selina's solipsism well.

jokes.text: To thank them for what? This Olympic-size swimming pool of shit that I'm doing the backstroke in right now?
jokes.setup: Sue tells Selina she has a drop-by to thank campaign volunteers.
jokes.punchline: Selina's self-pity explodes into an extended athletic metaphor — she's not just in a bad situation, she's competitively swimming in it.
jokes.explanation: The 'Olympic-size' specificity elevates this above a generic 'pool of shit' complaint. 'Doing the backstroke' adds the image of someone who is not just drowning but actively, performatively wallowing. Very Selina.

jokes.text: He loves all that fake smiley shit.
jokes.setup: Selina doesn't want to attend the volunteer brunch and suggests VP Tom James do it.
jokes.punchline: She projects fake cheerfulness onto Tom — revealing her own contempt for the performance of political warmth.
jokes.explanation: Competent character beat. The contempt for political performance is on-brand but not a standout line.

jokes.text: It's a veritable who's who of Wall Street. / Well, that sounds a lot sexier than my thing. Tell Tom that I get dibs on that.
jokes.setup: Mike mentions Tom James has a banking task force today.
jokes.punchline: Selina immediately decides to steal the meeting from her own VP purely because it sounds more glamorous. 'Dibs' is wonderfully childish for a president.
jokes.explanation: The word 'dibs' in the mouth of the President of the United States is doing all the work here. Excellent character integration — her impulsiveness and status-obsession colliding.

jokes.text: The president will be there shaking hands and thanking some of the volunteers for their hard work at some point in the near future. I'll get back.
jokes.setup: Gary is telling campaign volunteers (via phone/intercom) that the president will attend their brunch.
jokes.punchline: The language is so hedged and non-committal — 'some of the volunteers,' 'at some point in the near future,' 'I'll get back' — that it's a masterclass in saying nothing.
jokes.explanation: A good political communications joke — the language of non-commitment dressed up as an announcement. The escalating vagueness is well-executed.

jokes.text: What in the lunatic fuck is the master cleanse? Sounds like Nazi domestic policy.
jokes.setup: Mike announces he's on the master cleanse.
jokes.punchline: Selina's reaction escalates from bafflement ('lunatic fuck') to a dark historical analogy — the master cleanse sounds like Nazi Germany's regime of control over bodies.
jokes.explanation: The leap from 'master cleanse' to 'master race'-adjacent Nazi domestic policy is a perfectly executed dark analogy. 'Lunatic fuck' as an intensifier is a nice Veep construction. The Nazi pivot is unexpected but logical once you hear it.

jokes.text: Little known fact about the Nazis... their polling numbers within Germany, through the roof. Unbelievable numbers. Though also tragic. The Jewish demo couldn't have been that good.
jokes.setup: Selina's Nazi remark prompts Kent — the data analyst — to respond in his area of expertise.
jokes.punchline: Kent applies pure polling-analyst logic to Nazi Germany, then catches himself with 'though also tragic' before the devastating understatement: 'The Jewish demo couldn't have been that good.'
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's best joke and among the finest Veep has ever produced. Every element is perfect: (1) Character integration — only Kent, a data obsessive who views everything through a polling lens, could say this. (2) Structure — the mock-correction 'though also tragic' pretends at sensitivity before the devastating final line. (3) 'The Jewish demo couldn't have been that good' is an extraordinary understatement that makes the horror of the Holocaust legible through the banal language of focus groups. (4) The joke earns its dark transgression because it's satirizing the amorality of political data-think, not celebrating Nazism. Comparable to or exceeding the 9.5 anchor example.

jokes.text: Do you still take shits? / Not exactly take.
jokes.setup: Mike describes his master cleanse diet — water, maple syrup, lemon juice, cayenne pepper.
jokes.punchline: Selina asks the logical clinical question; Mike's answer ('not exactly take') implies something involuntary and horrifying, delivered with total calm.
jokes.explanation: Three words that imply everything without saying it. 'Not exactly take' is maximally economical — the audience's imagination does all the work. The deadpan delivery amplifies the comic horror. This is excellent joke economy, rare in sitcoms that usually over-explain.

jokes.text: Or you could email me at splett2@splettnet.net. Splett1 is my father. It'll be sad to see him go, but it'll be nice to get my hands on that handle, you know?
jokes.setup: In the middle of a formal recount legal proceeding, Richard Splett offers his email address.
jokes.punchline: The email domain 'splettnet.net' is absurd enough, but then Richard casually implies his father's impending death is mainly significant because it will free up the 'splett1' handle.
jokes.explanation: Richard Splett is one of Veep's greatest comedic creations and this is a standout Richard moment. The utter obliviousness — mentioning his father's death purely in the context of username availability — is perfectly in character. 'splettnet.net' is delightfully self-contained (he runs his OWN email server named after himself). The dark casual mention of the father dying is an afterthought, which is the whole joke. Exceptional character integration.

jokes.text: What is wrong with you, you Paddington Bear-looking fuck? You just gave them a Time Life instruction manual on how to fuck us.
jokes.setup: Richard has just helpfully explained legal strategies to their opponents in the recount meeting.
jokes.punchline: Dan's insult combines the weirdly specific 'Paddington Bear-looking fuck' with the dated reference of a 'Time Life instruction manual' — both perfectly placed.
jokes.explanation: The Paddington Bear comparison is inspired — specific, visual, and somehow apt for Richard's bumbling earnestness. 'Time Life instruction manual' is a beautifully dated reference. Two distinct images working together.

jokes.text: You guys have Michael Jordan sitting on the bench here, but you're starting Hakeem Olajutwat.
jokes.setup: Dan is continuing his rant about Richard being used instead of him.
jokes.punchline: The sports analogy flatters Dan as Jordan, but 'Olajutwat' — a portmanteau of Hakeem Olajuwon and 'twat' — is the actual joke.
jokes.explanation: A bravura piece of wordplay — 'Olajutwat' requires you to know who Hakeem Olajuwon is for the portmanteau to land, which makes it rewarding for the audience. Dan's self-aggrandizing logic is also perfectly in character. One of the episode's most quotable original coinages.

jokes.text: You are not Michael Jordan. You are a seven-foot-seven goony-looking Lithuanian who's gonna drop dead of Marfan syndrome.
jokes.setup: Dan has compared himself to Michael Jordan in the sports analogy.
jokes.punchline: Richard fires back with a perfectly specific counter-insult — seven-foot-seven, Lithuanian, Marfan syndrome — all real markers of a specific type of NBA player (Arvydas Sabonis/Rik Smits era), delivered with complete calm.
jokes.explanation: Richard's unexpected comeback is a great subversion — the bumbling Splett turns out to have a devastating specific counter-insult. 'Marfan syndrome' as the finishing blow is a wonderfully obscure medical detail. The specificity ('seven-foot-seven') is what elevates this.

jokes.text: I would never miss the opportunity to dry fuck Jim Whitman up the ass.
jokes.setup: Bob Bradley is being gracious and honorable about serving the president.
jokes.punchline: The statesman elder-figure's genteel manner is completely subverted by this graphically vulgar statement delivered with the same dignity.
jokes.explanation: The comedy is entirely in the gap between Bradley's patrician bearing and the graphic crudeness of the statement. The structure (noble setup, vulgar punchline) is clean Veep formula but well-executed.

jokes.text: All right, let's hear it, Affirmative Action Jackson.
jokes.setup: Bradley is meeting Amy's team and a Black team member speaks up with an observation about the mail-in ballots.
jokes.punchline: Bradley dismisses him with a casually racist nickname, revealing the 'legendary statesman' to be a creature of an older, uglier era.
jokes.explanation: The 'beloved elder statesman' being a casual bigot is a recurring Veep move (see also the nickname sequence) — it's character comedy that punctures nostalgia for a supposedly better era of politics.

jokes.text: Why aren't you moving on this, blondie? Right, Amy. Come on, get it together. My God in heaven.
jokes.setup: Amy has just explained the problem with the disqualified mail-in ballots.
jokes.punchline: Bradley's impatience with Amy — addressing her as 'blondie' while also being the one who needed the information explained — is both sexist and oblivious in equal measure.
jokes.explanation: Good character beat for Bradley's retrograde attitudes but not a standout joke in itself.

jokes.text: Well, Madam President, I've got big balls, but neither one of them are crystal.
jokes.setup: Selina asks Bradley for his honest gut feeling about whether they can win the recount.
jokes.punchline: Bradley's folksy refusal to predict uses the double meaning of 'crystal balls' while asserting his own virility in a single sentence.
jokes.explanation: A tight, well-constructed double entendre delivered with patrician confidence. 'Neither one of them are crystal' is excellent — grammatically playful and vulgar simultaneously. Very quotable.

jokes.text: He would say, 'Henry, you son of a bitch. I can lead a horse to water, but I can't milk it.'
jokes.setup: Bradley sets up a Nixon/Kissinger anecdote as if delivering profound wisdom.
jokes.punchline: The wisdom turns out to be a mangled proverb — 'lead a horse to water' and 'milk it' are from entirely different sayings — delivered as if it's profoundly meaningful.
jokes.explanation: Bradley confidently deploying a non-sequitur non-wisdom is a perfect character joke. The mangled proverb — where 'leading' and 'milking' are from two completely different saying — is absurd but the confidence of delivery sells it. Selina and the team's delighted laughter within the scene amplifies how great it is.

jokes.text: We called him Fatty Dicksuckle and B-B-Benny and the Jizz. Buttfucker. / Buttfucker, that's me. I can't believe you remembered my nickname.
jokes.setup: Ben recognizes Bradley; Bradley says he remembers Ben and uses affectionate nostalgia framing.
jokes.punchline: The 'million nicknames' turn out to be escalatingly obscene — 'Fatty Dicksuckle,' 'B-B-Benny and the Jizz,' and then the clincher: 'Buttfucker.' Ben's genuine pride in remembering his nickname is the final twist.
jokes.explanation: This sequence is masterfully structured. The escalation from 'Fatty Dicksuckle' to the Elton John parody 'B-B-Benny and the Jizz' to the final stripped-down 'Buttfucker' works as a three-beat escalation. But the real genius is Ben's response — 'Buttfucker, that's me. I can't believe you remembered my nickname.' Ben's PRIDE in this appalling nickname is the perfect button. The warmth is completely misplaced and that's the entire joke.

jokes.text: He hasn't worked in the White House since the late '80s. [beat] How old is Sue?
jokes.setup: Bradley greets Sue warmly as if she's a permanent fixture he remembers from long ago.
jokes.punchline: Kent's observation that Bradley hasn't been there since the '80s triggers Ben's existential question about Sue's age — implying Sue may have been there since the Eisenhower administration.
jokes.explanation: Three words — 'How old is Sue?' — contain the entire joke. The implication (Sue has been there since before any of them were born, perhaps immortal) is delivered with perfect economy. The beat between Kent's observation and Ben's question is everything. Running gag material that deepens the Sue mythology.

jokes.text: See that? Yeah, that's a comma. After fuck, that is a comma. So it doesn't say 'Fuck Selina Meyer.' It says, you know, 'Fuck, Selina Meyer!' That's a testament to this voter's earthy but unambiguous enthusiasm for President Meyer.
jokes.setup: The ballot reads 'Fuck Selina Meyer' and the Meyer team needs it to count.
jokes.punchline: Dan's grammatical reinterpretation — the comma transforms an insult into an enthusiastic endorsement — is absurdist legal sophistry delivered with complete sincerity.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's finest moments. The punctuation argument is genuinely clever — 'Fuck, Selina Meyer!' works grammatically as an enthusiastic direct address. 'Earthy but unambiguous enthusiasm' is a masterpiece of euphemistic political spin. This joke would work as a standalone piece of satirical writing. It captures everything great about Veep: absurd political desperation transformed into brilliantly sophisticated wordplay. The craft is exceptional and it earns every bit of its impact.

jokes.text: Great idea of yours. / Yeah, it is, isn't it? I'm looking forward to hearing what my next great idea is gonna be.
jokes.setup: Tom James is welcoming Selina to 'her' banking task force — the one she stole from him.
jokes.punchline: Selina's response acknowledges that she doesn't actually have ideas — she just takes credit for others' and waits for the next one to steal.
jokes.explanation: Selina accidentally revealing her intellectual dependency with total confidence is perfectly in character. 'I'm looking forward to hearing what my next great idea is gonna be' is a beautifully economical self-own.

jokes.text: I'm sure the Dow is rocketing skywards as we speak.
jokes.setup: Selina has turned Tom's policy meeting into a photo op.
jokes.punchline: Tom's deadpan sarcasm about the Dow 'rocketing' from a meaningless photo op is delivered with perfect composure.
jokes.explanation: Competent political satire. Tom's barely-contained frustration expressed through overly positive language is good but not exceptional.

jokes.text: Yeah, I'm gonna be pulling down Clint Eastwood cowboy movie style pussy in these things. Except I'm not the man with no name. I'm the man with mo' game.
jokes.setup: Jonah shows off his new cowboy boots to a colleague.
jokes.punchline: 'Mo' game' as a rhyming replacement for 'no name' — Jonah's delusional self-confidence married to a hip-hop inflection on a Clint Eastwood reference.
jokes.explanation: Pure Jonah Ryan — the delusion, the clueless machismo, the unnecessary rhyme scheme. 'Mo' game' is a genuinely fun coinage. His total confidence in his own attractiveness while discussing cowboy boots is on-brand.

jokes.text: Can Paul McCartney teach Kid Rock how to be a good songwriter? I mean, that's a bad example. Kid Rock rules, but...
jokes.setup: Jonah dismisses the idea that his canvassing technique can be taught.
jokes.punchline: The analogy positions Kid Rock as McCartney's equal in songwriting, then Jonah catches himself — but instead of acknowledging McCartney's superiority, he defends Kid Rock.
jokes.explanation: Exceptional character integration. Jonah's self-correction reverses the expected direction — a normal person would say 'bad example because they're not equally talented'; Jonah's correction is 'bad example because actually Kid Rock rules.' The self-awareness of 'that's a bad example' followed by the wrong takeaway is perfect comedy writing for this character.

jokes.text: I thought this was going to be two hours of Tom James lecturing us about being paid too much. / Well, Tom can be, dare I say... a bit of an asshat? / Actually, I was gonna say a champion for the middle class, but I kind of like your thing better.
jokes.setup: Selina is fishing for a way to bond with Baird over their shared dislike of Tom James.
jokes.punchline: Selina sets up a diplomatic 'dare I say' that Charlie completes crudely; Selina immediately abandons her diplomatic framing to validate the insult.
jokes.explanation: The misdirection is clean — 'dare I say' signals diplomatic hedging, then Selina immediately drops the pretense to validate the crude term. Her political calculation visible in real time is good character comedy.

jokes.text: Two grand? Where'd you find that? In your sofa seat cushion? / I think it was in my other pants, Madam President.
jokes.setup: Baird mentions he also donated to Selina's campaign. Selina notes the donation amount was $2,000.
jokes.punchline: Selina mocks the trivial sum; Charlie's response ('my other pants') matches her energy and implies his wealth is so vast that $2,000 is literally forgotten in coat pockets.
jokes.explanation: The two-line exchange is a tight mini-scene. 'My other pants' as a response to $2,000 is a perfectly pitched wealth flex that also reveals a quick wit — Baird is a match for Selina's needle. Good interplay.

jokes.text: Not in jail for what she did to you. / Honestly, it is an outrage. But I'm happy you're here.
jokes.setup: Selina is being introduced to Eli Park and reflexively asks about his wife Susan — who, it turns out, he's divorced from.
jokes.punchline: Rather than recovering gracefully, Selina doubles down by agreeing that whatever Susan did was 'an outrage' — with no knowledge of the actual situation.
jokes.explanation: Classic political cringe — the reflexive validation of a stranger's unknown marital grievance. 'Honestly, it is an outrage' is the peak: total conviction about something she knows nothing about.

jokes.text: Brooks Brothers sews prenups into all our suits. / Oh, are you divorced? / Oh, yeah, proudly. Yeah, best thing me and my husband ever did. Including our daughter.
jokes.setup: Selina has just survived the awkward Eli Park divorce moment.
jokes.punchline: Charlie's quip about Brooks Brothers and prenups leads to Selina casually revealing she considers her divorce better than her daughter.
jokes.explanation: Two jokes layered together. Charlie's Brooks Brothers/prenups line is sharp and witty. But Selina's 'best thing me and my husband ever did. Including our daughter' is the real payload — a devastating maternal indictment delivered casually. Perfect character integration: this is exactly who Selina is as a mother.

jokes.text: Yeah, best thing me and my husband ever did. Including our daughter.
jokes.setup: Selina reveals she's divorced too, calling it the best thing.
jokes.punchline: 'Including our daughter' — she rates the divorce higher than having her child.
jokes.explanation: Three words doing devastating work. The structure is perfect — 'best thing ever' is a complete sentence, then the addendum 'including our daughter' retroactively makes it a confession of maternal indifference. It's also true to Selina's character and her relationship with Catherine, which the show has established. High quotability and rewatch bonus because it recontextualizes every Catherine scene.

jokes.text: Some of your economic policies are what my friend Katie Gross likes to call fakakta.
jokes.setup: Charlie is delivering a polite but substantive critique of Selina's economic policy.
jokes.punchline: 'Fakakta' (Yiddish for 'messed up') deployed in the White House as a technical economic term, attributed to 'my friend Katie Gross.'
jokes.explanation: Setup for the Katie Gross connection joke. 'Fakakta' is a fun word choice that also characterizes Baird's background. Functional but primarily setting up the next beat.

jokes.text: Katie Gross? You know Katie Gross? I went to Smith with Katie Gross. I dated Katie Gross. / Yeah, so did I. / 'Cause I was at Smith. No, it's a Smith joke.
jokes.setup: Selina and Baird discover a mutual friend connection through Katie Gross.
jokes.punchline: Baird casually saying 'yeah, so did I' (implying he also dated a woman Selina dated) is the shock beat; Selina then explains 'it's a Smith joke' — implying same-sex relationships were common at the women's college.
jokes.explanation: The double-take structure is clean: Selina's revelation that she 'dated' Katie Gross, Baird's 'so did I,' the moment of confusion, then Selina's explanation that turns it into a winking lesbian joke about Smith. Well-constructed sequence.

jokes.text: You should read C.S. Lewis's book on grief. It's not as fun as 'The Lion, Witch, and the Wardrobe' series, but it's still pretty good.
jokes.setup: Richard and Amy are canvassing voters; they discover the second signatory on a ballot has passed away, and the widow mentions her husband.
jokes.punchline: Richard's grief counseling pivot — recommending C.S. Lewis to a bereaved widow — is classically Splett: sincere, thoughtful, and completely tone-deaf.
jokes.explanation: The joke has two layers: (1) Richard offering unsolicited grief counseling in the middle of a political canvass is the first layer. (2) The qualifier 'not as fun as The Lion, Witch, and the Wardrobe series' — as if that is a relevant consideration for a grief book — is the gem. The word 'fun' applied to C.S. Lewis's 'A Grief Observed' is the whole joke. Perfect Richard Splett delivery.

jokes.text: Jonah Ryan, Senior Deputy Recount Strategist for the Meyer campaign. Would it be all right if we waited for your husband inside? I think I have a small blister.
jokes.setup: Jonah is door-to-door canvassing while wearing his new cowboy boots.
jokes.punchline: Jonah's grandiose self-introduction is immediately deflated by the admission of a blister from his posturing footwear.
jokes.explanation: The callback to the cowboy boots pays off beautifully — Jonah's machismo accessory has literally injured him within the same scene. The pompous self-introduction followed immediately by the blister complaint is excellent deflation comedy.

jokes.text: Alav hashalom. Hebrew. / Maybe you should put on your regular shoes for a little bit.
jokes.setup: Jonah has said 'I'm sorry' to the widow about her husband; Richard chimes in with 'Alav hashalom' (a Jewish mourning phrase).
jokes.punchline: Richard's needless explanation — 'Hebrew' — followed by his completely practical response to Jonah's blister complaint, ignoring the grief moment entirely.
jokes.explanation: Richard's parenthetical 'Hebrew' — as if the widow needed this clarified — is good Splett characterization. The pivot to shoe advice is funny but not exceptional.

jokes.text: Well, I just came out West to play a little Cowboys and Injuns with Big Chief Suckum Choad here.
jokes.setup: Furlong arrives in Nevada and is greeted by Amy.
jokes.punchline: Furlong's greeting is a string of racist and obscene language deployed as friendly banter — vintage Furlong.
jokes.explanation: Standard Furlong transgression. The joke is purely character-based — his irredeemable offensiveness is the point. Not as crafted as the episode's best material.

jokes.text: Say it. / (CLEARS THROAT) How. / Me suck pee pee in teepee. / (LAUGHS) Atta girl.
jokes.setup: Furlong has brought his aide Will along. He demands Will say something.
jokes.punchline: Furlong has apparently trained Will to perform racist 'Native American' phrases on command. Will's resignation and Furlong's paternal 'Atta girl' complete the horror.
jokes.explanation: The horror of this exchange is in its structure: Furlong demands compliance, Will has clearly been broken, and 'Atta girl' is the perfect misogynistic topper that reveals the full depth of the power dynamic. This is Veep's darkness at its most sustained — not just Furlong's racism but his systematic degradation of his staff.

jokes.text: Step aside, roadkill. Big rig coming through.
jokes.setup: Furlong is pushing past Amy and Dan to get to the recount room.
jokes.punchline: His self-description as a 'big rig' when applied to the lanky congressman is absurd, and the dismissal of Amy as 'roadkill' is casual even by Furlong standards.
jokes.explanation: Generic Furlong swagger. Works as character beat but not among the stronger insults in the episode.

jokes.text: Hey, Grimace. What are you... no, no, no, not you. The other person in the room who looks exactly like Grimace.
jokes.setup: Furlong is trying to get the attention of someone in the recount room who apparently resembles the McDonald's character Grimace.
jokes.punchline: The reversal — 'not you,' implying there are TWO Grimace-lookalikes in the room — escalates the insult's scope.
jokes.explanation: The structural twist — 'not you... the other person who looks exactly like Grimace' — is what elevates this beyond a simple insult. The suggestion that there are two Grimace lookalikes in the room is absurdist and funny.

jokes.text: That is not an overvote. You need to trust me on this because I've been doing this since before your mother was throwing herself down the stairs belly first.
jokes.setup: Furlong is arguing with the O'Brien team about a ballot ruling.
jokes.punchline: The professional credential — 'I've been doing this since before...' — is completed by an image of horrific self-harm, making it a credential entirely dependent on the opponent's mother's history of violence.
jokes.explanation: This is exceptional Furlong material. The structure is brilliant: 'you need to trust me on this because I've been doing this since before your mother was...' sets up a standard 'I have experience' claim, then obliterates it with a shocking, specific, and bizarrely detailed image. 'Belly first' is the crucial detail that tips it from dark to genuinely funny. One of the episode's standout insults.

jokes.text: You want me to get you some glasses? Maybe I'll call in Mayor McCheese? He can come in here and explain election procedures to you.
jokes.setup: Furlong is continuing his attack on the opposing recount official — having already called him Grimace.
jokes.punchline: Mayor McCheese as the expert on election procedures is a perfect escalation of the McDonald's character theme — now expanding the insult universe while making the specific point about election knowledge.
jokes.explanation: The callback to McDonald's characters is well-constructed — Grimace leads to Mayor McCheese, which has the added bonus of being the character with 'Mayor' in the name (ironic given they're arguing about elections). The escalation of the insult universe is a satisfying comedic structure.

jokes.text: (WHIMPERING) / Oh, I'm not... Tracy. Look, it's... you're wearing purple. All right? It's got nothing to do with your body shape or your... the way your head goes into your shoulders so smoothly.
jokes.setup: The O'Brien official apparently starts crying after Furlong's sustained abuse.
jokes.punchline: Furlong's attempted comfort is itself an ongoing series of fresh insults — clarifying that the purple clothing, not 'your body shape,' nor 'the way your head goes into your shoulders so smoothly' is the issue.
jokes.explanation: One of the best Furlong moments in the episode. The structure is perfection: he tries to APOLOGIZE but the apology generates new and more specific insults in the process of denial. 'The way your head goes into your shoulders so smoothly' is the standout — it's a precise anatomical observation dressed as a non-insult, which makes it devastating. The 'so smoothly' is the killer modifier. This is exceptional cringe comedy craft.

jokes.text: Special or normal? / Well, they seemed normal, but I'll confirm.
jokes.setup: Selina is told she has a meeting with Olympic medalists.
jokes.punchline: Selina's question about whether the Olympic athletes are 'special or normal' (presumably 'Special Olympics' vs. standard Olympics) is casually dehumanizing; Gary's response — 'they seemed normal, but I'll confirm' — takes the question completely seriously.
jokes.explanation: A very efficient joke. Selina's question is appalling and Gary takes it at face value — both aspects are perfect character comedy. The economy is excellent. 'I'll confirm' implies Gary will go and inspect the athletes to determine their disability status, which is itself horrifying and funny.

jokes.text: So you catch 'The Godfather' on TV last night? / No. / There was nothing like seeing it in the theater when it first came out back in '72. / I don't care for movies.
jokes.setup: Bob Bradley attempts to make small talk with Sue by asking about a movie.
jokes.punchline: Sue's response — 'I don't care for movies' — is delivered with such finality that it obliterates Bradley's nostalgia entirely.
jokes.explanation: Four words — 'I don't care for movies' — that are perfect Sue. The delivery must be bone-dry and the substance is bizarre (who doesn't 'care for' movies as a category?). Bradley's enthusiastic '72 nostalgia is completely vaporized. This is great deadpan reaction comedy — Sue as an inhuman fixture of the White House.

jokes.text: So congratulations on your gold medal. / It's bronze.
jokes.setup: Selina is meeting Olympic medalists and congratulates one on their gold medal.
jokes.punchline: The athlete corrects her — it's bronze, not gold. Selina's complete lack of preparation (or interest) is exposed.
jokes.explanation: Functional cringe gag. The gold/bronze correction is a common type of political gaffe humor. Works but not distinctively Veep material — similar jokes appear in any political satire.

jokes.text: Fuck am I supposed to do with this? / It'd be nice for your library.
jokes.setup: Selina has received a kayak paddle or piece of athletic equipment as a gift from the medalist.
jokes.punchline: Gary's suggestion that the gift 'would be nice for your library' — i.e., the presidential library — is both practical and absurd.
jokes.explanation: Good Gary beat — his instinct is always to protect and contextualize Selina's awkwardness. The library suggestion is funny for its incongruity but not a major joke.

jokes.text: They opened wide and slurped it down, ma'am.
jokes.setup: Selina asks what the press is saying about the banking task force.
jokes.punchline: Mike's metaphor for the press accepting the narrative is graphic oral sex imagery delivered as a routine press update.
jokes.explanation: Vulgar press metaphor standard for Veep. Functional but Mike's best material in the episode is elsewhere.

jokes.text: The extended 'banking task force' exchange as a thinly veiled desire for another meeting with Charlie Baird.
jokes.setup: Selina keeps insisting she wants 'another banking task force'; Gary keeps finding politic reasons to resist.
jokes.punchline: The repeated euphemism 'banking task force' clearly means 'another date/encounter with Charlie Baird' — the subtext is entirely transparent to the audience but maintained throughout the exchange.
jokes.explanation: This extended running gag works on multiple levels: (1) It's a sustained euphemism joke where 'banking task force' is transparently a desire to see Baird. (2) Gary's increasing understanding — culminating in 'if you want, I can arrange a more discreet banking task force' — shows him catching on. (3) Selina's 'I don't want THAT kind of banking task force' is the perfect button — she's protesting her own subtext. This is sophisticated structural comedy across multiple lines.

jokes.text: Ma'am, if you want, I can arrange a more discreet banking task force. / I don't want that kind of banking task force. I want the banking task force that I want.
jokes.setup: Gary has caught on to the subtext of the 'banking task force' requests.
jokes.punchline: Gary's euphemistic offer to arrange something 'discreet' is met with Selina denying that any subtext exists — while making it absolutely clear through her specificity that it does.
jokes.explanation: The button on the running gag is excellent. 'I want the banking task force that I WANT' — the redundant insistence, the italicized WANT — is Selina at her most petulant and self-deluded simultaneously. Gary's understanding and her denial creates a perfect ironic dynamic.

jokes.text: Oh, just hang up on her. Wait, where is Catherine? She should be getting this for her little movie.
jokes.setup: The team celebrates the recount news; Amy is still on the conference call.
jokes.punchline: Selina dismisses Amy callously while suddenly wanting Catherine's camera — contradicting her constant 'Catherine, out' dismissals.
jokes.explanation: A good callback that shows Selina's inconsistency — she's been dismissing Catherine all episode but now wants her. The hypocrisy is character comedy.

jokes.text: I'm feeling light-headed. That bottle's my only source of blood sugar.
jokes.setup: The team is celebrating the recount news; Gary is hovering protectively over a bottle.
jokes.punchline: Kent — the data analyst — has apparently also been on the master cleanse and is now suffering; his clinical self-diagnosis delivered with complete flatness is very Kent.
jokes.explanation: A decent callback to the master cleanse subplot. Kent on the cleanse is funny in concept; the light-headed admission is well-delivered but not exceptional.

jokes.text: Chung will do it. / Chung would volunteer for a beheading video to get national airtime.
jokes.setup: They need surrogates for Sunday talk shows.
jokes.punchline: Selina's line about Chung volunteering for a 'beheading video' for national exposure is a dark riff on political ambition that reduces ISIS-style terrorism to a career move.
jokes.explanation: Sharp political commentary on the intersection of media hunger and terrorism. The joke works because it's not really about terrorism — it's about political ambition. Economically delivered.

jokes.text: Can you go tell him to fuck a bag of glass or something?
jokes.setup: Tom James has arrived for an unannounced meeting.
jokes.punchline: 'Fuck a bag of glass' as a dismissal is notable for its physical specificity — the violence of the image elevates it above a generic 'go away.'
jokes.explanation: Excellent vulgar economy. 'Bag of glass' is a specific and vivid image. The 'or something' at the end is a nice qualifier that suggests this is the least bad option Selina can think of.

jokes.text: Actually, I told him something similar to that, but he's most insistent.
jokes.setup: Selina tells Gary to dismiss Tom James rudely; Gary reports having already done something similar.
jokes.punchline: Gary has apparently already told the VP of the United States to do something obscene, yet reports this with bureaucratic composure.
jokes.explanation: Gary reporting that he's already insulted the VP and the VP is 'most insistent' anyway is a great beat. The formality of 'most insistent' against the implied vulgarity of what he said is perfect.

jokes.text: So, economy czar, kids' birthday parties, reflecting pool boy.
jokes.setup: Tom James is performing elaborate supplication, offering to do anything to help.
jokes.punchline: The escalation from 'economy czar' (dignified VP role) to 'kids' birthday parties' to 'reflecting pool boy' traces a trajectory of deliberate self-humiliation.
jokes.explanation: The three-part list is brilliantly constructed. 'Economy czar' establishes him as important; 'kids' birthday parties' is the first humiliation; 'reflecting pool boy' — evoking a cabana/pool boy — is the perfect degradation endpoint. Economy is excellent (seven words for the triple). Tom's self-awareness and dignity in the delivery make it funnier.

jokes.text: Just don't ask me to be the United Nations ambassador 'cause I still have some pride.
jokes.setup: Tom has just offered to do virtually anything, including 'reflecting pool boy.'
jokes.punchline: The UN Ambassador — a prestigious diplomatic post — is somehow beneath being a 'reflecting pool boy' in Tom's hierarchy, which reveals how awful the UN job is perceived to be.
jokes.explanation: A standout joke. The inverted prestige hierarchy — UN Ambassador below pool boy — is a precise, original observation about the UN's perceived irrelevance in Washington. It works as political commentary AND as Tom James character comedy (his pride in specific areas despite offering total capitulation). Very quotable.

jokes.text: Oh, by the way, I just polished the floor in the Eisenhower Room. Be careful in there, all right?
jokes.setup: Tom is leaving after his show of submission.
jokes.punchline: Rather than simply exit, Tom adds that he's already been performing menial tasks — having actually polished the floor — escalating his self-abasement into genuine action.
jokes.explanation: The callback and escalation are satisfying — Tom hasn't just offered menial labor, he's already done it. 'Be careful in there, all right?' — the domestic concern for others' safety — is the perfect button.

jokes.text: Why did Tom James just wrap up his balls in butcher paper and put them in my purse?
jokes.setup: Tom has just left after his elaborate show of submission.
jokes.punchline: Selina's image for Tom surrendering his autonomy — 'wrapped up his balls in butcher paper and put them in my purse' — is a vivid extended metaphor for political emasculation.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's most quotable lines. The specificity of 'butcher paper' elevates this from a simple emasculation metaphor — it implies the balls were carefully wrapped, as if a delicacy being prepared. The image is complete, surprising, and perfectly captures the power dynamic. Selina's gift for vulgar imagery at its best.

jokes.text: Because he thinks you're gonna win Nevada. / Tom thinks I'm gonna win Nevada? / 'Nev-add-a.' He's the smartest guy in DC.
jokes.setup: Ben explains Tom's submission by saying Tom thinks Selina will win Nevada.
jokes.punchline: Selina's correction of Nevada's pronunciation — insisting on 'Nev-ADD-a' — is a callback to the episode title and a well-known political pitfall for out-of-state politicians.
jokes.explanation: The pronunciation correction is a real thing politicians get wrong in Nevada and it's used well here — Selina's insistence on the correct pronunciation while still only caring about winning is good character comedy.

jokes.text: Hey, I'm gonna be president, so I can go take a shit in the Rose Garden if I want to. / We used to call that a Jimmy Carter.
jokes.setup: Selina is excited about possibly winning Nevada and begins to brag about presidential privilege.
jokes.punchline: Gary's instant response — 'We used to call that a Jimmy Carter' — reframes presidential defecation in the Rose Garden as a named political tradition.
jokes.explanation: Gary's response is extraordinary. The joke works on three levels: (1) It implies Gary has insider knowledge of presidential bathroom habits. (2) It implies Jimmy Carter specifically defecated in the Rose Garden. (3) The use of the past tense 'we used to call' suggests this is shared White House staff lore. The specificity of Jimmy Carter (not Clinton, not Reagan) is perfect — he's the president most associated with homespun ordinariness. Very quotable.

jokes.text: I warn you, I had a pretty comprehensive White House tour on my fifth grade class trip. I actually know a lot about West Wing history if you'd like... no.
jokes.setup: Selina offers Charlie a private West Wing tour.
jokes.punchline: Charlie's self-deprecating claim that he already knows the tour is immediately and completely shut down by Selina's single 'no.'
jokes.explanation: Selina's dismissive 'no' cutting off Charlie mid-sentence is economical comic deflation. The setup (his earnest fifth-grade tour expertise) makes the one-syllable response funnier.

jokes.text: I feel like that guy doesn't like me very much. / Who, Gary? / Yeah. / Come on, that's like saying that the cat doesn't like you or that table doesn't like you.
jokes.setup: Charlie notices Gary's hostility toward him.
jokes.punchline: Selina's response — equating Gary to a cat or a table — denies his humanity while technically defending him. The comparison to a 'table' is the perfect escalation.
jokes.explanation: An exceptional Selina/Gary joke. The escalation from 'cat' (a pet — sentient but not really) to 'table' (inanimate object) perfectly captures Selina's view of Gary. 'Or that table doesn't like you' is the killer addition. Simultaneously dismissive of Gary AND subtly devastating about Selina's inability to recognize the people who care for her. One of the episode's best character moments.

jokes.text: Now there's this Rockwell down here that is so stunning and I swear to God if I lose this fucking election, I'm gonna stick it in my suitcase and I'm taking it with me.
jokes.setup: Selina is giving Charlie a West Wing tour and pointing out the art.
jokes.punchline: Her genuine appreciation for the Rockwell ends with a plan to steal it if she loses — expressed with complete sincerity and no apparent awareness that this is a crime.
jokes.explanation: Selina casually describing art theft as a post-election contingency plan is perfectly in character. The transition from appreciation to larceny in a single breath is great.

jokes.text: Some beach house. / Not sure which beach.
jokes.setup: Selina says she has Rockwells and asks where they are.
jokes.punchline: Charlie's casual inability to remember which beach house contains major artworks is the exact right kind of wealthy obliviousness.
jokes.explanation: Five words maximum impact. 'Not sure which beach' is the perfect elaboration of the obliviousness — not just one beach house but apparently several, and the Rockwells could be in any of them. Very economical wealth satire.

jokes.text: This is the actual closet where Warren Harding fathered a child with his teenage mistress. / They left that off our fifth grade tour.
jokes.setup: Selina is showing Charlie notable White House history spots.
jokes.punchline: Selina presents this as a genuine tour highlight; Charlie's deadpan confirms the elementary school omission is understandable.
jokes.explanation: The callback to the fifth grade tour is satisfying. The Harding presidential scandal turned into a flirtatious tour stop is good comedy — Selina using White House history as seduction material.

jokes.text: Fuck, today. / Now, was that a vote in favor of today? / Oh, that was a fuck, comma, today, yes!
jokes.setup: Selina and Charlie are about to enter her private quarters; she exclaims 'Fuck, today.' (apparently at the key card).
jokes.punchline: Charlie immediately invokes the comma argument from the recount ballot — 'Fuck, comma, today' as enthusiastic endorsement rather than expletive.
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's best joke and one of the finest callbacks in Veep's run. It takes the 'Fuck, comma' argument from the recount room — an earlier joke already scored highly — and applies it to a romantic context with perfect spontaneity from Charlie. The callback rewards attentive viewers, it's perfectly timed, it integrates both characters, and 'Oh, that was a fuck, comma, today, yes!' from Selina's delighted confirmation is the ideal button. Structurally impeccable. Impact in a group viewing would be enormous — the delayed recognition laugh, the delight of the callback. This is what a 9+ callback looks like.

jokes.text: Sophie, haven't seen you in years. How are your illegitimate children?
jokes.setup: Dan is greeting Amy's sister Sophie after years apart.
jokes.punchline: The social nicety 'How are your...' completed with 'illegitimate children' rather than a normal inquiry.
jokes.explanation: The social form — 'How are your X?' — inverted to inquire about illegitimate children is a nice structural joke. Very Dan — he deploys social conventions as weapons.

jokes.text: I thought that Carson City was, like, part of Las Vegas. / Vegas is 432 miles away. / Sorry I'm not a 'where everything is' nerd.
jokes.setup: Sophie complains about Amy's hotel in Carson City.
jokes.punchline: 'Sorry I'm not a where everything is nerd' — Sophie's dismissal of geographic knowledge as a specific type of nerdery is unexpectedly funny.
jokes.explanation: 'Where everything is nerd' is an original, funny coinage. The idea that knowing Nevada geography is a form of nerdy excess is delightful and recognizably millennial in its logic.

jokes.text: Were you two just about to bone?
jokes.setup: Sophie has walked in on what was clearly about to be a Dan/Amy hookup.
jokes.punchline: The directness of 'bone' — unvarnished, casual, with no attempt at social grace — is the entire joke.
jokes.explanation: Effective but not crafted. The word choice is appropriately crude for Sophie's character but the joke is purely in the directness, not any structural cleverness.

jokes.text: What happens in Vegas, it stays in Vegas. / We're not in Vegas.
jokes.setup: Sophie is encouraging Amy and Dan to continue their hookup.
jokes.punchline: Sophie applies the 'what happens in Vegas' logic to Carson City; Amy pedantically corrects the geography (432 miles off).
jokes.explanation: A nice callback to the Vegas distance argument, with Amy's pedantry now deployed against Sophie's logic. Works as character comedy but the setup/punchline is fairly obvious.

jokes.text: You know, I hardly ever did that with President Hughes. And even when we did, I was just kind of going through the motions.
jokes.setup: Selina and Charlie are in post-coital conversation in the residence.
jokes.punchline: Selina's comparison of Charlie favorably to a previous president she apparently also slept with — while casually implying she was bored during — is spectacularly inappropriate and revealing.
jokes.explanation: This is extraordinary post-coital cringe. The revelation that Selina also slept with President Hughes is delivered as a compliment to Charlie (I liked this better!) which makes it simultaneously flattering and appalling. 'Going through the motions' with a sitting president is a devastating detail. Selina's sociopathic oversharing perfectly in character.

jokes.text: Yeah, I'm not that kind of a president.
jokes.setup: Charlie is being ushered out after their encounter; Selina wants to maintain a presidential image.
jokes.punchline: Having just described sleeping with a previous president 'going through the motions,' Selina insists she's 'not that kind of president' — the irony being she has just demonstrated precisely that she is.
jokes.explanation: The irony is thick and well-constructed — Selina's self-delusion in the immediate aftermath of demonstrating the opposite is peak character comedy.

jokes.text: You come from a big family, do you? / I do come from a big family. I do, I do, I do. / (LAUGHS) It's actually just me. I'm an only child.
jokes.setup: Gary is escorting Charlie out and making small talk with forced enthusiasm.
jokes.punchline: Gary's over-eager agreement ('I do, I do, I do') about something false — he's not from a big family — revealed by Charlie's correction is a great Gary beat.
jokes.explanation: Charlie's bemused correction of Gary's false agreement is a nice beat. Gary's triple 'I do, I do, I do' — agreeing before he hears the question properly — is perfectly in character for someone who reflexively validates everyone around Selina.

jokes.text: Oh, so soon?
jokes.setup: Selina tells Gary to take Charlie to the southeast gate.
jokes.punchline: Gary's 'Oh, so soon?' — implying he wants Charlie to leave sooner, not later — is a passive-aggressive reaction to being used as a chaperone for Selina's rendezvous.
jokes.explanation: Three words carrying enormous Gary subtext. His passive-aggressive jealousy of Selina's attention given to Charlie is barely concealed. The inversion — 'so soon?' when he clearly wants Charlie gone — is a neat ironic reversal.

jokes.text: Well, what you have here is a classic two fires, one glove situation. And you need to know which hand to put the glove on.
jokes.setup: Selina asks Bradley for strategic advice about where to focus recount resources.
jokes.punchline: Bradley's 'wisdom' is another mangled, nonsensical metaphor — two fires, one glove makes no logical sense as a strategic framework.
jokes.explanation: Callback to Bradley's nonsensical wisdom dispensing. 'Two fires, one glove' is even more incoherent than 'lead a horse to water but can't milk it.' The team's continued acceptance ('That's exactly right. / Exactly.') is the perfect topper — they've given up questioning him.

jokes.text: No, oh, wait a minute. You mean Nevada. / What did I say? / You said Nebraska. / Well, I'll get that one for you, too.
jokes.setup: Bradley is wrapping up and refers to flying to 'Nebraska' when he means Nevada.
jokes.punchline: Bradley's 'Well, I'll get that one for you, too' — rather than correcting himself, he promises to deliver Nebraska as an additional prize — is a masterpiece of oblivious confidence.
jokes.explanation: This is an exceptional joke that reveals the full scope of Bradley's decline without cruelty. 'I'll get that one for you, too' — confidently promising to deliver a state he accidentally mentioned — is brilliant character comedy. It's not confused fumbling; it's shameless doubling down. The economy is perfect and the character specificity (only an elder statesman of this type could say this with such certainty) makes it extraordinary.

jokes.text: Buttfucker. / Bye, Bob.
jokes.setup: Bradley is leaving; the team says goodbye.
jokes.punchline: Ben uses his old nickname as a sincere farewell — now the appalling nickname functions as genuine warmth.
jokes.explanation: The callback landing as a genuine farewell is structurally satisfying. 'Buttfucker. / Bye, Bob.' — the juxtaposition of the obscene nickname delivered with affection and 'Bye, Bob' as the warm response — is a great button on the Bradley subplot.

jokes.text: You know, I was over in the East Wing and I saw a painting of Sue holding George Washington's horse.
jokes.setup: The team is nostalgically praising Bradley after his departure.
jokes.punchline: Selina offhandedly reveals there is a historical painting of Sue holding Washington's horse — extending the joke that Sue has been a White House fixture since before anyone's memory.
jokes.explanation: An exceptional callback to the 'How old is Sue?' joke. The George Washington painting escalates the gag to its logical absurd conclusion — Sue is literally in an 18th-century painting. Delivered casually mid-conversation as if it's a normal White House detail. The specificity ('holding George Washington's horse' rather than just being in a painting with him) is the right detail. Very Veep — the absurdism is completely deadpan.

jokes.text: Charlie Baird is a friend of mine. Charlie Baird is one of the great financial minds of our time and would be an asset to any administration.
jokes.setup: Charlie Baird goes directly from Selina's private quarters to a press conference for O'Brien.
jokes.punchline: His description of Selina as 'a friend' while appearing at her opponent's press conference is political code for 'I just slept with you and I'm already campaigning against you.'
jokes.explanation: 'A friend' doing so much political damage — the understatement of calling your most recent sexual partner 'a friend' while appearing for the opposition — is good ironic comedy.

jokes.text: You're kidding. / No. / He fucked me and then he fucked me? / What, is he trying to fuck me?
jokes.setup: Selina watches Charlie endorse O'Brien on TV immediately after their encounter.
jokes.punchline: The triple repetition of 'fucked me' — moving from sexual to political and back — is a devastating triple-meaning construction.
jokes.explanation: The triple 'fucked me' is a bravura piece of Veep writing. The first use is sexual, the second political, the third an interrogative about future intent. The word does three jobs in two sentences. The escalation structure is tight and Selina's outrage playing on both registers simultaneously is perfect character comedy. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: You went straight from here to O'Brien's hotel? / Are you kidding me? Did you take a shower at least in between?
jokes.setup: Charlie has come back to apologize; Selina confronts him about going to O'Brien's team.
jokes.punchline: Selina's concern about whether Charlie showered between their encounter and O'Brien's hotel is grotesquely practical — both a genuine indignity and a completely inappropriate inquiry.
jokes.explanation: A great cringe beat. The shower question is both petty and oddly practical — Selina's mind going immediately to the personal logistics of betrayal. Very specific and character-true.

jokes.text: Were you thinking about his cabinet while you were fucking me? Seriously. Were you fantasizing I had some sort of a goatee and was on the wait list for a liver?
jokes.setup: Charlie explains the O'Brien meeting was pre-scheduled.
jokes.punchline: Selina's sexual jealousy manifests as a specific physical description of O'Brien (goatee, liver transplant waitlist) — reducing a political rival to a grotesque fantasy figure.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's finest jokes. The 'goatee and wait list for a liver' description of O'Brien as a sexual fantasy is so specifically wrong — and so revealing of Selina's contempt for him — that it becomes transcendently funny. The construction is perfect: the general ('some sort of a goatee') followed by the hyper-specific medical ('on the wait list for a liver'). This is Veep at its peak — combining political satire, sexual jealousy, and grotesque physical specificity in a single sentence. Exceptional craft.

jokes.text: You didn't think it was a conflict... first of all, he doesn't even have a cabinet. Look, if you win the election, it doesn't mean anything anyway. / If O'Brien wins the election... / Okay, let's hear what this is. / ...you've slept with the Secretary of the Treasury.
jokes.setup: Charlie is defending his O'Brien meeting by saying that if she wins, it doesn't matter.
jokes.punchline: The conditional — 'if O'Brien wins, you'll have slept with the Secretary of the Treasury' — reframes the encounter as a form of political leverage for Charlie, which is both impressive and appalling.
jokes.explanation: The structure is excellent — the interrupted sentence building tension ('if O'Brien wins...') to the punchline that reframes the encounter as Charlie's insurance policy. Selina's 'that's classy' button is perfect.

jokes.text: SELINA: Oh, that's classy. This is over. / Over? I didn't even know it was a thing. / Absolutely not a thing. And if it was a thing, it's over.
jokes.setup: Selina declares 'this is over.'
jokes.punchline: Charlie's 'I didn't even know it was a thing' leads to Selina first agreeing it's 'not a thing' — then immediately threatening that the not-thing is over. The logic collapses in on itself.
jokes.explanation: This exchange is structurally excellent. 'This is over' → 'I didn't even know it was a thing' → 'Absolutely not a thing. And if it was a thing, it's over.' The triple movement — denial, meta-denial, and the re-affirmation as ending — is a perfect piece of logical absurdism rooted in character. Selina wanting to end something she won't even admit existed is pure self-delusion comedy.

jokes.text: Uh, he... he's a Charlie Baird.
jokes.setup: A reporter asks Mike what the nature of Selina's relationship with Charlie Baird is.
jokes.punchline: Mike's complete press secretary meltdown ends with the tautological answer 'he's a Charlie Baird' — a non-answer so complete it's almost philosophical.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's most memorable moments. The entire press briefing builds to the total communicative collapse of 'he's a Charlie Baird.' The tautology — using the name to define the person — is the perfect endpoint of Mike's disintegration. It's specific to Mike's character (his incompetence as press secretary), structurally the right length (the pause before it is everything), and exceptionally quotable. The craft is in the buildup and the inevitable deflation.

jokes.text: Looks like Charlie Baird is fucking the president.
jokes.setup: Mike's disastrous press conference has ended; a reporter turns to a colleague.
jokes.punchline: The reporter's conclusion — delivered as a factual press take — uses 'fucking' in both the sexual and political sense, deliberately echoing Selina's 'he fucked me' triple construction from earlier.
jokes.explanation: A good callback that closes the episode's central double-entendre. The reporter's deadpan delivery of what is simultaneously accurate reporting and crude gossip captures the Veep worldview perfectly.

jokes.text: You know, I got my sister her own room. So it's just little old you in that big old room.
jokes.setup: Dan is making his move on Sophie after Amy has left.
jokes.punchline: Dan's predatory smoothness — having arranged the sister's hotel room as a separate seduction move — is delivered with his characteristic shameless confidence.
jokes.explanation: Dan being Dan — smooth, calculating, and immediately pivoting from Amy to her sister. Character comedy that works but isn't exceptionally crafted.

jokes.text: Everybody here is so boring. / The shit you do is such bullshit. / Remind me again what it is that you do that's so interesting. / I work at CVS.
jokes.setup: Sophie is dismissing the recount world as boring; Dan challenges her.
jokes.punchline: 'I work at CVS' — the anti-climactic revelation after all the edgy positioning is a perfect deflation.
jokes.explanation: The setup (edgy, dismissive, clearly positioning herself as interesting) ends with 'I work at CVS.' The gap between posture and reality is the joke. The total economy — four words — is excellent. It also sets up the CBS confusion joke.

jokes.text: Really? CBS? I would love to work at CBS. / Oh, God, don't be a dick. / No, I'm serious. There's always openings. Do you seriously think that you could get me something? / Maybe late night. / Late night's perfect!
jokes.setup: Sophie says she works at CVS; Dan mishears or pretends to mishear as CBS.
jokes.punchline: Dan's pivot from seduction to career networking — apparently willing to overlook the CVS/CBS confusion entirely if there's a CBS contact in it — reveals his real priorities. Sophie's 'Late night's perfect!' accepting the fantasy is the button.
jokes.explanation: This sequence is excellent character comedy for Dan. The CVS/CBS confusion functions as Dan refusing to accept the deflating reality — he needs her to work at CBS so he can use her. His pivot from seduction to networking is pure Dan Egan sociopathy. Sophie's enthusiastic buy-in ('Late night's perfect!') implies she's as delusional as he is. Their compatibility is expressed through mutual delusion.

jokes.text: 23 messages? / A nightcap?
jokes.setup: Ben is reviewing his phone after the celebration; the final scene of the episode.
jokes.punchline: The implication is that Bob Bradley — the dignified elder statesman — has sent 23 messages and is now texting Ben (presumably 'Buttfucker') to ask for a nightcap, completing the portrait of a man living in an earlier, less restrained era.
jokes.explanation: A perfect button on the Bradley subplot. The tag scene (or near-tag) of Ben scrolling through 23 messages from the legend he called 'Buttfucker' and getting a nightcap invitation is a satisfying closer. The gap between Bradley's statesman image and '23 messages + nightcap text' is the joke. Economy is superb — five words for the whole thing.

tmdb_overview: Amy, Dan, Jonah and Richard try to win the Presidency for the President. Back in D.C., Selina brings in respected Washington fixture Bob Bradley, aka "The Eagle," and, after nudging out Tom James on the newly created Banking Task Force, meets billionaire banker Charlie Baird. Meanwhile, Mike tries to get healthy before his baby arrives; Kent investigates Sue's age; and Amy and Dan share a charged moment.
insight: Nevada campaign leg plants 81 jokes in 37 minutes, surviving political chaos on character comedy alone.
